problem_summary: 2025 MCM/ICM Problem B (Sustainable Tourism, discrete/optimization type). The task: build an optimization model for sustainable tourism in Juneau, Alaska, that balances economic, social, and environmental concerns; design a plan to use the additional revenue from tourism measures and show how it feeds back into sustainability; run a sensitivity analysis on key factors; adapt the model to another crowded destination and to low-tourist-volume areas; and write a one-page memo to the Juneau Tourist Council. This paper frames the deliverable as a "Tourism Sustainable Management Plan (TSMP)" and recommends capping visitors at ~1.43M/year, raising visitor fees ~72.4 USD/person, and adjusting the hotel tax rate by ~0.08.
models_used: Model I — Multi-Objective Optimization Model: three conflicting objectives (max Financial Revenue FR, min Environmental Damage ED, max Resident Satisfaction RS) over decision variables x_i (daily visitors per attraction), x_0 (daily visitor cap), Delta t_i (fee increase), Delta r (hotel-tax increase), S (road area), G (waste-handling capacity)
FR objective: closed-form revenue = sum(x_i*t_i) + x_0*Delta t + x_0*k*(r+Delta r), with attraction fees t_1=239, t_2=189, t_3=160 and hotel spend k=$179, base tax r=0.09

ED objective: nonlinear regression of temperature vs visitors (logarithmic fit T=0.9286 ln(0.0019641 x_0)+5.5043) and carbon footprint vs visitors (quadratic/exponential fit), normalized by historical minima and weighted sum (w=0.7 temperature, 0.3 carbon)

RS objective: AHP-weighted composite of residents' income (0.50), urban congestion measured as road area per tourist (0.34), and infrastructure/waste pressure (0.16); 2023 satisfaction anchored at 68 points (38% positive + 30% neutral) as a normalization baseline
AHP (Analytic Hierarchy Process) with a pairwise-comparison matrix seeded from external survey item-total correlations (Ko & Stewart, Jeju Island: 0.64/0.44/0.20) to set RS sub-weights

Tourism demand-elasticity constraint (Nguyen ASEAN model): ln x = beta0 + beta1 ln Y + beta2 ln t + beta3 ln S + eps, calibrated to ln x = -0.72 ln(243+Delta t+173 Delta r)+4.43 linking fee/tax increases to visitor numbers
NSGA-II (Non-dominated Sorting Genetic Algorithm II) solved in Python to produce a 255-solution Pareto front; authors hand-picked a balanced solution and reported a 4-solution optimal cluster

Model II — Expenditure-Feedback Model: allocates extra revenue Delta FR = x_0(Delta t + k Delta r) to road area S and waste capacity G in a 6.5:1 ratio (from RS correlation coefficients), with a two-case feedback rule comparing prior-year surplus Delta FR_{t-1} against current cost
Model III — Maldives expansion: re-parameterized same MOO+NSGA-II framework (drops road-area term, t_0=$351, r=0.12, etc.) plus a low-tourist-volume qualitative adaptation
Sensitivity analysis via local (one-at-a-time) perturbation, +/-10% on each decision variable, summarized as a correlation heatmap between decision variables and objectives
key_techniques: Translating a vague 'balance economy/society/environment' prompt into three explicit, quantifiable, conflicting objective functions — the core move that makes the problem a tractable MOO

Borrowing external empirical parameters instead of inventing them: Ko & Stewart survey correlations to seed the AHP matrix, Nguyen's elasticity ratios (|beta1/beta2|≈2.3, |beta2/beta3|≈100) to set demand-curve coefficients — gives the model literature-anchored credibility despite no provided data
Data self-sourcing: explicitly listing collection databases (traveljuneau.com, NOAA, budgetyourtrip) since the problem provided none

Using NSGA-II's Pareto front plus a documented selection rule (reject high-ED solution, reject low-revenue solution, keep the balanced one) to convert many solutions into one defensible recommendation

Closing the loop on the 'additional revenue' sub-question by reading NSGA-II output: noticing S and G decision variables sit at their upper bounds, then building the expenditure-feedback model specifically around those two levers
Normalization/anchoring tricks: dividing by 2023 baseline values and historical minima so heterogeneous indicators (USD, °C, tCO2e, road area) become comparable, dimensionless objective terms
Adding a constraint penalty mechanism to vanilla NSGA-II to enforce the environmental/demand constraints
Sensitivity heatmap as the evidence vehicle: ranking decision-variable importance (x_0 dominant, fees > hotel tax, x_3/rainforest diversion matters) directly feeds the memo recommendations

Generalization-as-validation: re-running the identical pipeline on the Maldives and surfacing an interpretable difference (RS more sensitive to visitor numbers because Maldives is tourism-dependent) rather than just claiming 'it works'
why_it_won: From a judge's perspective this paper wins on coverage, coherence, and defensibility rather than mathematical depth. It answers every sub-question explicitly and in order (MOO model -> revenue-use plan -> sensitivity -> other destination -> low-volume areas -> memo), and the parts are genuinely linked: the NSGA-II output (S, G at upper bounds) motivates Model II, and the sensitivity ranking (cap is king, fees beat hotel tax, divert to rainforest) directly becomes the four memo recommendations. The choice of NSGA-II is well-justified via a literature-review decision tree comparing it against epsilon-constraint, weighted-sum, MOPSO, etc. Crucially, with no data given, the team grounded almost every parameter in a cited external source (AHP weights from a Jeju survey, demand elasticity from an ASEAN study, EPA waste figures), which reads as rigor rather than hand-waving. The expansion to the Maldives doesn't just restate the model — it extracts a meaningful, explainable contrast, demonstrating genuine generalizability. The narrative framing (TBMP -> TSMP, 'tourism never ends') gives the paper a memorable throughline that judges remember.
section_highlights: Strong, conventional MCM structure: titled summary sheet that states all three models and the concrete numeric recommendation up front; explicit Assumptions-with-Justifications; a Notations table; a Model Preparation section that documents data self-collection. Two flowcharts do heavy lifting — a literature-review decision tree (Figure 2) justifying the NSGA-II choice, and a full work-flow diagram (Figure 3) mapping every section to a model component. The objective-function build-up uses a repeated 'Modelling ideas -> Supplementary assumptions -> Model construction' micro-template for FR/ED/RS, which makes the derivations easy to follow. The Pareto front (Fig 9) plus a three-solution comparison table with an explicit reject/keep rationale shows decision-making, not just computation. The sensitivity heatmap (Fig 10) is the analytical centerpiece and is verbally translated into ranked, actionable conclusions. The one-page memo is concise, non-technical, and maps each recommendation back to a model finding. Weaknesses section is honest (ignored seasonality, attraction interdependence, alcohol/housing indicators).
pitfalls_or_limitations: Notational and equation sloppiness: equation (8) RS simplification has terms that don't dimensionally reconcile with (7); several equations are mislabeled (two '(4)'s, a constraint cross-referenced as '(4)' and '(5)'); RS sub-weights are written as w3/w4/w5 but never cleanly tied to the AHP 0.50/0.34/0.16 in the simplified form

Parameter calibration is hand-wavy and partly arbitrary: 'tentatively assume |beta2|=|beta3|', then beta2=beta3=100 contradicts the stated |beta2/beta3|≈100 ratio; the 6.5:1 S:G spend ratio and the 0.7/0.3 environmental weights are asserted with thin justification
Single 2023 data point used as the regression/normalization anchor; carbon and temperature fits rest on ~7-8 points with pandemic years dropped, so the nonlinear fits are fragile and overfit-prone

Local one-at-a-time perturbation (+/-10%) ignores interaction effects between decision variables — and the model itself has strong couplings (e.g., x_0 drives FR, ED, and RS simultaneously), so the sensitivity ranking may understate joint effects

Internal inconsistency between cases: in Juneau fees matter more than hotel tax, but the Maldives section claims 'controlling the hotel tax brings more benefits than increasing tourist fees' — presented as a feature but reads as a possible sign of unstable conclusions
Assumption 4 (daily visitors always hit the cap, turning an inequality into equality) is convenient but strong and is leaned on heavily throughout

Several figures are low-fidelity/garbled in the extracted form (NSGA-II flowchart has a malformed node; Maldives heatmap has out-of-range values like 1.80/1.88), suggesting the original visuals were rough
Units/scale confusion in the summary (visitor fee '72.4 USB', x_0 sometimes in millions/year vs k/day) that a careful judge would notice
reusable_patterns: Decompose an ambiguous 'balance X, Y, Z' prompt into 3 explicit conflicting objective functions + decision variables + constraints, then solve as MOO with NSGA-II and present a Pareto front — a reusable skeleton for any tradeoff/policy problem

When no data is provided, make data self-sourcing an explicit, table-documented step and borrow parameters from cited domain literature (surveys for weights, econometric studies for elasticities) instead of guessing
Seed an AHP pairwise-comparison matrix from published item-total correlations of an analogous case study — a fast, defensible way to set subjective weights
Normalize heterogeneous indicators by dividing by a fixed baseline year and/or historical extremes so they combine into a single dimensionless objective

Read the optimizer's output for structure (which decision variables hit their bounds) and let that finding drive the next sub-model — turns a 'use the extra revenue' sub-question into a principled allocation model
Use a sensitivity heatmap not as decoration but as the bridge from analysis to recommendations: rank variable importance, then write each memo bullet against the top-ranked levers
Validate generalizability by re-running the identical pipeline on a second, structurally different case and explaining one interpretable difference, rather than asserting universality
Justify the chosen algorithm with a literature-review decision tree that visibly compares the alternatives you rejected (epsilon-constraint, weighted-sum, MOPSO, etc.)
Frame the whole paper around a memorable acronym/narrative arc (TBMP -> TSMP) and lead the summary sheet with the concrete numeric recommendation
